speaker: interviewer
text: You do not see Steve. You do not see the camera. You look straight, and I look straight at you. So this way you'll be perfect-- Today is December 10, 1996. I will be interviewing Sonia Burstyn, nee Waldman. The interviewer is Tina Tito. We are in Carle Place, New York in the USA. The language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 10, 1996. I am Tina Tito, the interviewer, and I will be interviewing Sonia Burstyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Burstyn, can you please tell me your full name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Burstyn. S-O-N-I-A B-U-R-S-T-Y-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waldman. W-A-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, Stadniki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-A-D-N-I-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that village near? What big town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Ostrog and bigger town, Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me the name of your father and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abba, A-B-B-A. Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gittle, G-I-T-T-L-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Truchler, T-R-U-C-H-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters and brothers. If you will start with your oldest sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Mamcia, M-A-M-C-I-A. Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married with two children. And her married name was Charbash, C-H-A-R-B-A-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her two children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your next sibling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Buzik-- Jewish name Itzhok-- B-U-Z-I-K, Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was married to Bronia, B-R-O-N-I-A. They had two children. One is Ruth, R-U-T-H, and the other one was Shoshona, S-H-O-S-H-O-N-A. May I say that she was killed? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your next sibling after Itzhok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sibling is Gene, Gene Waldman Tyrangel, G-E-N-E W-A-L-D-M-A-N, Tyrangel, T-Y-R-A-N-G-E-L. And next is going to be me. [LAUGHS] And then my smaller sister, which is Mania, M-A-N-I-A and Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N. Married name is Jarkon, J-A-R-K-O-N. She had-- has two children. One is [NON-ENGLISH], and the other one is [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] lives in Israel, and his second name is Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jarkon. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Jarkon. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jarkon. J-A-R-K-O-N. And his sister, [NON-ENGLISH] Jarkon, J-A-R-K-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another brother, Sammy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Sammy is older than Mania, sorry. And he's Shamay, S-H-A-M-A-Y, and Waldman, W-A-L-D-M-A-N. He changed his name from Waldman to Yaari. So he's-- right now his name is Yaari, Y-A-A-R-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had other siblings that died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were 12 children, and six were alive, and six died from different diseases and accidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get now to your hometown, Stadniki. And about your parents, were they born in Stadniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is a third generation born in Stadniki. My mother is-- was born in-- gee, I forgot the town. She was born--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mizoch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mizoch. Mizoch. It's M-I-Z-O-C-H, Mizoch. And they were living up 'til they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the professions of your grandparents who lived in Stadniki? Your father's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as my father's. He was in the mill. And Grandma is a housekeeper. We did have another house in Ostrog. The other house we had a nanny and a housekeeper. And all four children were studying in Ostrog. And these two women were supervising us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then on vacations and holidays, we used to come to visit our parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to school in Stadniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until fourth grade. Then I went to Ostrog. And the school was Kazemirza, K-A-Z-E-M-I-R-Z-A, and up till the 1938 war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the German-- the Russians occupied our town. And after settling down, we went to Ostrog and continued the education in Russian and Ukraine languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Polish and Jewish. Very little Jewish. Mostly Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you understand Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish? Very well. I know the language to write, read, and I studied it. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents, did they read Jewish press, Polish press? What was the language that they enjoyed reading in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly Jewish. But they were very well acquainted with the Polish news and politics and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the village of Stadniki? How many people in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say close to 1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was four Jewish families. Three families Waldmans, and one is another family that moved to Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when it came to a Saturday or a Friday night for praying, how did you work it out in Stadniki with four families? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly my mother lit candles. We were not very religious but observed all the holidays and all the little things about Jewishness. And Saturday usually were the meals, chicken soup and whatever goes with it. And all the children were there. But since we were studying in Ostrog, so only for the holidays, vacations, and other little intermissions from studying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a synagogue in Stadniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They used to get together in one house. They used to call neighboring villages. And they used to pray and do everything for the holidays. Preparations and so on were all like a collective thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Ostrog, was there a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A very big one. As a matter-- one, one is-- was-- the Ostrog synagogue was known as the biggest synagogue in Wolyn. Very beautiful and very lovely decorated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an Orthodox synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just in between. Not very religious, not very--

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of living in Stadniki and again in Ostrog, what was your relationship with the community at large, with the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were like one of them. We were like a family. We grew up together. We went to school together. We played together. We ate in each other houses and slept in each other houses. So we were very, very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Including my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were-- in terms of holidays, did they enjoy the holidays with you? Did they understand the Jewish rituals and customs in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very respectful toward us. So did we. We tried even-- we were allowed-- to celebrate their holidays. So we really were very close with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the schools, the same kind of situation occurred with the teachers? Respect?

speaker: interviewee
text: In village-- the four years that I studied there, definitely. It was very, very respectful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Ostrog, how big was the town of Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 30,000. Out of the 30,000, over 10,000 Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we had Jewish Hebrew schools. We had a high educated people and education centers. And very well known also city because it was a historical city. So we had a, a lot of historical places to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were also integrated into the society, into the culture, of Ostrog, the non-Jewish culture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't feel before the war anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes we did. Oh, yes. From strangers, definitely. In schools with strangers. On street with strangers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot, a lot of incidents that happened to young people. To the older people maybe I know less because I was too young. But there was a lot of anti-Semitic accidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like Hebrew school children would go out for an intermission and play in the, in the schoolyard. So the non-Jewish children would come out from the other side and throw stones and, and sometimes even hurt people. In schools there were name calling, because children really didn't fight that severely. But always just because you were Jew, you had to be in the back. Because you were Jew, you could not attend certain school. There was one school, [NON-ENGLISH], and you could not possibly get into that school because you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a public school. And other things-- little things, because we tried to live in our ghettos with the Jews, so it wasn't that bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of the government administration in Stadniki and Ostrog, were there Jews within the government representation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ostrog, yes. In Stadniki, no. We were too little of a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that Jews could hold any position they wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. No. Very, very limited. Very limited. But some of them were such geniuses that they couldn't help it but hire them.

speaker: interviewer
text: If people wanted to go on to higher education, where did they go to get their education?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our Ostrog, we had gymnasium and lyceum, and even Hebrew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] college. But mainly we used to go to Lwow, to bigger cities to have higher yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had a higher education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did have. I believe he had a few years of college. My mother did not. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The rest of the family? Of course. My oldest sister, she did finish, as I remember, high school and then higher. They had teachers. The teachers came, and there were about three children or four as a group. He lived in Stadniki, and he taught them higher education. As far as my brother's concerned, Mother and Father felt that a boy should know more than a girl, I guess, because he did go to some higher, like college, and it was in Russian. I can't connect it, but it was in Russian. So here we are. He finished his college. As far as going down to my older sister, Gene, she finished college-- gymnasium. And she did go to a school during the Russian occupation for a teacher. It was pedagogy institute. So she finished that. Then myself, I did go to school, and I did finish two years of college and, again, privately picked up when I came to America, USA. I did go to business college. It's Cambridge School of Business on 42nd Street in Manhattan. So I did finish there this college and from then on started to work. But coming back to my brother, he had a Jewish education. He started in Hebrew school in Ostrog. Went to-- during the war-- after the war, went to college in higher education in Israel. The same thing happened to my sister. She started with Hebrew school and went to Israel to higher education there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Politically, in terms of thoughts about Israel, what was your parents' belief in terms of Zionism, in terms of having a state of Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were supportive. But they were not neurotic and crazy about it. I mean, they knew that they are Jewish people and they have to support Jews and help them as brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong, belong to any Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did belong to, gee, got me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar. And I worked there with other groups and had been there until the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other activities that you did-- sports activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Starting with water, swimming, kayaking, bicycle riding, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] horseback riding, that was my love-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little tennis and ball playing, something that we learned in school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about, I think, all. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were very outdoors? Participated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, skiing and skating. I forgot. That was my love, too. Participating, yeah. I always, in school, they used to choose me, because I wasn't lazy, to be the leader in some kind of games.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of social person were you in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tried my best to be one of everybody because it seemed like we were well off and better off than other children, I would say, in my class, my school. So I tried to hide it kind of. Blend in to be one of them. So I learned it in the early years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever travel out of the area? Ever on a vacation somewhere out of the area of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little because we had such a beautiful place where we lived in Stadniki that people used to come to us in vacationing. Because we had water, we had beautiful, beautiful trees around, and the healthiest atmosphere, I would say. The healthiest place, I think. And we loved it. And we used to come just for the holidays, so it wasn't enough even for us. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we really didn't go except to go to Rovno to visit people or other villages or so. But we didn't travel too much. In terms of the professions in town, what were the jobs or the work that Jews did in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a student. So mainly schools and up until the war and during the war, even. It's later during the war that I had to work very hard physically. And that's about all. Otherwise, I did not have any profession really.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the town itself-- the stores-- who were they owned by? The stores.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say [SIGHS] more by Jews. I would say a good 80% Jewish stores. I mean, it's my opinion. I might be wrong in the 20 different ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: But even though it may have had a majority of Jewish stores, the relationship with the population was a healthy one you think? A good one?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ostrog?

speaker: interviewer
text: In, in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how healthy because I was too young to understand. The Jew still was a Jew and a dirty Jew and, and they belong in ghetto and whatever it is. And go to Israel, and-- it, it was anti-Semitic atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- was it the same atmosphere in Stadniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Stadniki it was our hometown. We were one of them. We spoke-- I'm sorry. We spoke fluently Polish and Ukraine. I still do. And just because of that, I think it was a big plus for us. Every place we went-- like we went to school-- nobody could really detect my Jewish accent. So I was accepted faster than anyone with a Jewish accent. So we had the plus. And Ukrainians the same thing. There is not a slight Jewish accent because we spoke it so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work of the Ukrainian people in town? What kind of jobs did they hold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly they had their own little houses, little outskirt. They, they, they were mingling with the Jewish peddlers and selling. But I would say their main [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] work was the farms and around the farms, selling everything from the farms and dealing with Jews, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the biggest flea market, I would call it, or market, was, I guess, in Ostrog. Because people from all over-- and when I say people, mostly Ukrainian, Polish people-- non-Jewish-- used to come and bring all their goods and sell and trade and sometimes you see them in the movies, you know? Like horses and things like that. So very big one. In Stadniki you say that there was a Ukrainian population and a Polish population.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the division in terms of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish population was very small. It was like Jewish population. They had maybe five families Polish. And the, the rest is Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Poles treated well in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did because we had-- I failed to mention to you-- a, a Polish family that practically owned Stadniki. I forgot to tell you, which is very important. She was one lady, two sisters, single. They were the ones to employ these people, give them, I guess, bread and butter. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of her being there, they better watch out with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the family? The Polish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was young people, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. And they didn't have any father. So they had only a, a mother, and her name was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And their family name? Their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the mayor of the town, who ran the town, what was his background? Was he Polish? Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian. Ukranian. He was Ukrainian. And most of the time, he took orders from that lady, from that Polish lady that owned this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she also own the mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Mogenitska. OK. Their name were Mogenitska's Estate. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father work for her when he worked in the mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. The mill-- everything belonged to her, all right. And he did not-- he rented it. He didn't work for her. He rented it and pay rent for the ground, I guess, I guess, the-- every-- all the buildings and including the mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the transactions were fair? They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was treated well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in very good relationship. Very good. So there we didn't have any problem, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move forward. And tell me about the time that the Russians came into your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Russians came in into the town. They were looking for very rich people. So when they came to us, they are right away is how rich are we? Well, they found out that we were not. And if we did, then they didn't find out. [LAUGHS] So my father still was operating the mill. We still were living in our borders, and we all went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did not threaten your life in any way, the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. From the beginning, maybe a little bit because they didn't know us. It's the rich people. They did get Mogenitska out of the-- her place and ruined her beautiful house with books and everything else, I remember, and sent them to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other people were taken to the town-- to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with us. No. These [INAUDIBLE] were the only ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog? A lot of people. As a matter of fact, two of our neighbors-- actually, one neighbor-- at night they took them and took them to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were not Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people wealthy. Very wealthy. They lived practically next door to us. So that I remember very well. As far as the other people, I know that there was a lot of people that were. And some of them willingly left Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape. Thank you.
